title.value: An Overview of Diagnostic Captioning: Current Status and Future Directions
title.evidence: An Overview of Diagnostic Captioning: Current Status and Future Directions

authors.evidence: John Pavlopoulos1,* Vasiliki Kougia1,2, Ion Androutsopoulos2, and Dimitris Papamichail3

year.evidence: This article is an extensive overview of DC.
year.notes: Assumed publication year based on context.

abstract.value: Diagnostic Captioning (DC) concerns the automatic generation of a diagnostic text from a set of medical images of a patient collected during an examination. DC can assist inexperienced physicians, reducing clinical errors. It can also help experienced physicians produce diagnostic reports faster. Following the advances of deep learning, especially in generic image captioning, DC has recently attracted more attention, leading to several systems and datasets. This article is an extensive overview of DC. It presents relevant datasets, evaluation measures, and up to date systems. It also highlights shortcomings that hinder DC’s progress and proposes future directions.
abstract.evidence: Diagnostic Captioning (DC) concerns the automatic generation of a diagnostic text from a set of medical images of a patient collected during an examination. DC can assist inexperienced physicians, reducing clinical errors. It can also help experienced physicians produce diagnostic reports faster...

doi.notes: No DOI provided in the paper.

problem_statement.value: The increased workload in medical imaging leads to a higher likelihood of errors in diagnostic reporting, necessitating tools that assist radiologists in generating accurate reports efficiently.
problem_statement.evidence: An increased workload in this demanding task increases the likelihood of medical errors (e.g., when radiologists are tired or pressured). These errors are not rare...

method_type.evidence: DC methods usually employ the encoder-decoder architecture... retrieval-based approaches, where text from similar previous exams is reused...
method_type.notes: Combination of encoder-decoder and retrieval-based methods highlighted.

architecture.value: Encoder-decoder architecture with potential incorporation of retrieval-based methods.
architecture.evidence: The most common system architecture in DC is the encoder-decoder approach. However, retrieval-based methods have also been proposed for DC...
architecture.notes: Highlights the dual approach in DC.

data_sources.evidence: In this work, we focus on the latter two quality datasets, MIMIC-CXR and IU X-RAY...

experiment_design.value: Evaluation of various DC methods using different datasets and metrics.
experiment_design.evidence: We provide a segmentation of current DC systems by the kinds of methods they use, also reporting evaluation scores for each system with all available measures.
experiment_design.notes: Focus on comparative analysis of methods.

evaluation_metrics.evidence: We have pointed out, however, that DC aims to report only information that helps in a medical diagnosis... The results we collected may also help other researchers produce results that will be more directly comparable...
evaluation_metrics.notes: Metrics used include both traditional and clinical measures.

key_findings.value: Retrieval-based methods often outperform complex encoder-decoder systems in clinical recall, indicating the importance of leveraging previous diagnostic texts.
key_findings.evidence: Even the simple nearest neighbor approach... has been reported to outperform all other approaches in clinical recall.
key_findings.notes: Highlights the effectiveness of retrieval-based methods.

statistical_significance.evidence: We also note that not all of the results of Table 4 are directly comparable...
statistical_significance.notes: Statistical significance is implied through comparative analysis.

value: Ability to leverage large datasets for improved accuracy.
advantages.evidence: Tools that would help radiologists produce higher quality reports... could have a significant impact.
advantages.notes: Advantages are aligned with the goals of DC.

value: Current datasets may not be fully representative.
Evaluation measures often fail to capture clinical correctness.
Reliance on automated tagging systems can lead to inaccuracies.
limitations.evidence: Evaluation measures employed by previous DC research mainly assess lexical overlap... which can lead to cases where a clinically wrong generated report can be scored higher than a clinically correct one.

value: Development of hybrid explainable methods combining generation and retrieval.
Improvement of evaluation metrics for clinical correctness.
future_work.evidence: Our main findings also guide our proposals for future work on DC, listed below.

provenance.section_header: Conclusions and Directions for Future Research

future_work.notes: Future work aims at addressing current limitations.

code_availability.notes: No code repository linked in the paper.

dataset_availability.value: Datasets are available for IU X-RAY and MIMIC-CXR.
dataset_availability.evidence: In this work, we focus on the latter two quality datasets, MIMIC-CXR and IU X-RAY...
